---
title: "The Gradient as a Direction in Space"
subtitle: "Core Sample · Geometry of Data"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from mpl_toolkits.mplot3d import Axes3D  # noqa: F401

# One restrained visual style for every figure in this article, matched to the
# rest of the Geometry-of-Data track.
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (6.2, 4.0),
    "figure.dpi": 140,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.25,
    "font.size": 11,
    "axes.titlesize": 12,
    "lines.linewidth": 2.0,
})

VEC    = "#2b3a67"   # generic vectors / points
GRAD   = "#c1121f"   # the gradient / the answer direction
ACCENT = "#0a7d4d"   # highlights / tangent moves
GREY   = "#6c757d"
PALE   = "#9fb3d1"


def draw_vec(ax, tail, head, color=VEC, lw=2.4, label=None, z=5,
             label_offset=(0.0, 0.0), ls="-"):
    """Draw a 2-D arrow from tail to head with a clean head."""
    ax.annotate("", xy=head, xytext=tail,
                arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-|>", color=color, lw=lw,
                                shrinkA=0, shrinkB=0, linestyle=ls), zorder=z)
    if label is not None:
        ax.text(head[0] + label_offset[0], head[1] + label_offset[1], label,
                color=color, fontsize=13, ha="center", va="center", zorder=z + 1)
```

> **Where this sits in the track.** This is the second article of **Movement VI — Calculus
> on Vectors**, and it is where the gradient stops being a column of numbers and becomes a
> *direction in space*. It assumes [GEOM-22], which built the **partial derivative** as
> "vary one coordinate, freeze the rest," stacked the partials into the **gradient**
> $\nabla f$, and proved the first-order approximation $f(x+\Delta x)\approx f(x)+\nabla
> f^\top\Delta x$. It also leans hard on [GEOM-04]: the **dot product** $u^\top v = \sum_i
> u_iv_i$, the **norm** $\|u\| = \sqrt{u^\top u}$, the relation $u^\top v =
> \|u\|\|v\|\cos\theta$, and the **Cauchy–Schwarz inequality** — these supply the entire
> geometric payoff. From the floor it needs only single-variable differentiation, restated
> as used. The one question: *why does the gradient point in the direction of steepest
> ascent, and why is it perpendicular to the level sets?* It sets up [GEOM-24] (the
> Hessian, which decides *which kind* of critical point $\nabla f = 0$ is) and [GEOM-25]
> (the gradient and Hessian of a quadratic form). It is the **geometric foundation that
> the Optimization track builds gradient descent on** — but the algorithm itself belongs
> there; here we own the geometry, not the iteration.

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

[GEOM-22] handed us the gradient as a stacked column of partial derivatives. That is an
algebraic object; this article reveals it as a *geometric* one. We define the
**directional derivative** $D_u f = \nabla f^\top u$ — the slope of $f$ as you walk in a
chosen direction $u$ — and ask which direction makes that slope largest. The Cauchy–Schwarz
inequality from [GEOM-04] answers in one stroke: the slope is maximized exactly when $u$
points along $\nabla f$, so **the gradient is the direction of steepest ascent**, and its
length is the steepest slope itself. We then prove the gradient is **perpendicular to every
level set** — walk along a contour, where $f$ does not change, and you are walking at right
angles to $\nabla f$. Finally we read off the **first-order optimality condition**: at a
peak, a valley, or a saddle the gradient must vanish, $\nabla f = 0$, because no direction
can be uphill. The single claim the article defends is that **the gradient is not merely
"the vector of slopes" — it is the one arrow that simultaneously tells you which way is
steepest uphill, how steep that is, and which way the surface is flat**, and that all three
facts are consequences of one inequality about dot products.

# The Core {#sec-core}

::: {.callout-important title="The Core"}
The gradient $\nabla f(x)$ is the arrow in input space that points in the direction of
**steepest ascent** of $f$ at $x$, and whose **length** $\|\nabla f(x)\|$ is the rate of
climb in that steepest direction. Equivalently: among all unit directions $u$, the
directional slope $D_u f = \nabla f^\top u$ is largest when $u$ aligns with $\nabla f$.
A direct corollary is that $\nabla f$ is **perpendicular to the level set** through $x$
(the contour where $f$ is constant), since along that contour $f$ does not change at all.
And where there is no uphill direction — a peak, a pit, or a saddle — the gradient must be
the zero vector, $\nabla f = 0$: the **first-order optimality condition**.
:::

Everything below orbits that box. We already have the gradient as a list of partials from
[GEOM-22]; the work of this article is to show that this particular list of numbers, read
as an arrow, carries three pieces of geometry at once — a *direction* (steepest uphill), a
*magnitude* (how steep), and an *orthogonality* (perpendicular to the flat directions). The
engine that delivers all three is a single fact about dot products, Cauchy–Schwarz, which
we restate and use rather than assume.

# Why we are doing this: a slope is not enough, we need a *direction* {#sec-why}

Keep one question in view the whole way through: **once a loss depends on many parameters,
"downhill" is no longer obvious — so which way do we actually step?** In Calc 1 the answer
took no thought: a function of one variable has a slope $f'(x)$, and there are only two ways
to move — you step left or right according to the sign of $f'(x)$. With $n$ parameters there is no single slope. Stand on a hillside in
the parameter landscape of [GEOM-22] and you can face any of infinitely many compass
directions; each gives a different slope. To descend you must pick a direction, and to pick
well you must know *which direction is steepest*. That is the question this article exists
to answer, and the gradient is the answer.

## The problem that forces it

The history here is the history of optimization itself. In 1847 Augustin-Louis Cauchy,
faced with solving large systems of equations arising in astronomy and geodesy — the same
over-determined problems that birthed least squares (the story of [REGR-01]) — proposed a
strikingly simple idea: to minimize a function of several variables, repeatedly take a small
step in the direction in which the function *decreases fastest*. He called it the *méthode
générale*. The whole method rests on one sub-problem he had to solve first: *of all the
directions you could step, which one decreases the function fastest?* Cauchy's answer — the
negative of the gradient — is the geometry we derive below. Every modern training loop, from
linear regression to a billion-parameter network, is a descendant of that 1847 idea, and
every one of them depends on the gradient meaning "steepest" in the precise sense we are
about to pin down.

There is an older thread too. Carl Friedrich Gauss and others, mapping the Earth, drew
**contour lines** of constant elevation, and surveyors knew by instinct that water runs
downhill *perpendicular* to the contours — the steepest path crosses the level lines at
right angles. That folk-geometry is exactly the theorem "$\nabla f \perp$ level set" we
prove in @sec-perp. We are formalizing what map-readers have known for two centuries: the
direction of steepest change is square to the lines of no change.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"You already have the gradient from the previous article — what is left to do? Isn't
'steepest ascent' just the definition of the gradient?"* No, and this is a subtle point an
interviewer uses to separate memorization from understanding. In [GEOM-22] the gradient was
*defined* as the stack of partial derivatives — slopes along the coordinate axes only. That
definition says nothing, on its face, about *off-axis* directions or about which direction
is steepest. The content of *this* article is the theorem that this particular stack of
axis-slopes turns out to govern the slope in *every* direction (via the dot product), and
that the steepest of all those directions is the gradient's own. "Steepest ascent" is a
*derived property* of the gradient, not its definition — and the derivation is the
Cauchy–Schwarz argument below.
:::

# Intuition before symbols {#sec-intuition}

Picture the loss surface of [GEOM-22] once more: a landscape with height $f(x_1,x_2)$ above
the floor. Stand at a point. You may step in any direction $u$ along the floor. Each choice
gives you a slope — walk toward the uphill side and the slope is positive and large; walk
along the hillside (neither up nor down) and the slope is zero; walk downhill and it is
negative. There is a single direction that is *steepest uphill*, the way a marble would roll
if you reversed gravity. **The claim is that this steepest direction is the gradient.**

Now overlay the contour map — the level sets, the curves where $f$ is constant, like the
elevation lines on a topographic map. Walking along a contour, your height never changes, so
the slope in that direction is exactly zero. The steepest direction, which has the *largest*
slope, ought to be as far from "zero slope" as possible — and the geometry that makes that
precise is: **the steepest direction is perpendicular to the contour.** Water crossing
elevation lines at right angles is the everyday picture. @fig-intuition draws a surface, its
contours, and the steepest-ascent arrow, so you can see the gradient sitting square to the
level curves and aimed straight uphill before we prove a thing.

```{python}
#| label: fig-intuition
#| fig-cap: "A surface, its contour map, and the gradient. **Left:** the surface $f(x_1,x_2)=\\exp(-(x_1^2+x_2^2)/3)\\cdot(x_1+1)$, a tilted hill. **Right:** its contour map (level sets). At the marked point the red arrow is the gradient: it points in the direction of steepest ascent (toward higher contours, uphill on the left) and it crosses the level curve at a right angle. The two facts proved in this article — steepest ascent and perpendicular-to-contour — are visible here before any algebra."

def f(x1, x2):
    return np.exp(-(x1**2 + x2**2) / 3.0) * (x1 + 1.0)

def grad_f(x1, x2):
    e = np.exp(-(x1**2 + x2**2) / 3.0)
    df1 = e * (1.0) + (x1 + 1.0) * e * (-2 * x1 / 3.0)
    df2 = (x1 + 1.0) * e * (-2 * x2 / 3.0)
    return np.array([df1, df2])

g = np.linspace(-3, 3, 80)
X1, X2 = np.meshgrid(g, g)
Z = f(X1, X2)

fig = plt.figure(figsize=(9.0, 4.2))
axL = fig.add_subplot(1, 2, 1, projection="3d")
axL.plot_surface(X1, X2, Z, cmap="viridis", alpha=0.8, linewidth=0, antialiased=True)
axL.set_xlabel("$x_1$"); axL.set_ylabel("$x_2$"); axL.set_zlabel("$f$")
axL.set_title("the surface $f(x_1,x_2)$")
axL.view_init(elev=30, azim=-120)

axR = fig.add_subplot(1, 2, 2)
cs = axR.contour(X1, X2, Z, levels=14, colors=PALE, linewidths=1.0)
p = np.array([0.6, -0.9])
gp = grad_f(p[0], p[1])
gpu = gp / np.linalg.norm(gp) * 1.0
draw_vec(axR, (p[0], p[1]), (p[0] + gpu[0], p[1] + gpu[1]), color=GRAD,
         label=r"$\nabla f$", label_offset=(0.18, 0.22))
axR.scatter([p[0]], [p[1]], color="black", s=35, zorder=6)
axR.set_xlabel("$x_1$"); axR.set_ylabel("$x_2$")
axR.set_title("contours + gradient (steepest, $\\perp$ contour)")
axR.set_aspect("equal")
plt.tight_layout()
```

That is the destination. The rest of the article earns it: we define the slope-in-a-direction
precisely (the directional derivative), prove it equals $\nabla f^\top u$, then use one
inequality to find the steepest $u$ and one short argument to show $\nabla f$ is square to
the contours.

# The formal development {#sec-formal}

## The directional derivative: slope along any direction {#sec-directional}

In Calc 1, the derivative measured the rate of change as you nudged the single input. With a
vector input we must say *which way* we nudge. Fix a point $x \in \mathbb{R}^n$ and a
direction $u \in \mathbb{R}^n$. Walking from $x$ in the direction $u$ traces the straight
path $x + tu$ as the scalar $t$ runs through small values: at $t=0$ we are at $x$, and
increasing $t$ moves us along $u$. The rate at which $f$ changes as we walk is an ordinary
one-variable derivative of the height-along-the-path.

::: {#def-directional}
**Definition (directional derivative).** The **directional derivative** of
$f:\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}$ at the point $x$ in the direction $u\in\mathbb{R}^n$ is
$$
D_u f(x) = \lim_{t\to 0}\frac{f(x + tu) - f(x)}{t},
$$ {#eq-dirderiv-def}
the rate of change of $f$ as we move away from $x$ along $u$, provided the limit exists.
:::

Compare @eq-dirderiv-def with the Calc-1 derivative
$h'(a) = \lim_{t\to 0}\frac{h(a+t)-h(a)}{t}$ from [GEOM-22]: they are *the same limit*. If we
define the one-variable helper $\varphi(t) = f(x + tu)$ — the height of $f$ as we slide along
the path — then @eq-dirderiv-def is exactly $\varphi'(0)$. The directional derivative is the
Calc-1 slope of the curve you get by slicing the surface along the line through $x$ in the
direction $u$. The partial derivatives of [GEOM-22] are the special case where $u$ is a
coordinate axis: taking $u = e_j$ (the vector with $1$ in slot $j$ and $0$ elsewhere) makes
@eq-dirderiv-def into @eq-partial-def of [GEOM-22], the slope along the $j$-th axis. So the
directional derivative *generalizes* the partials from the $n$ axis directions to *all*
directions.

A word on a convention that will matter when we talk about "steepness." The number $D_u f$
scales with the length of $u$: if you double $u$ you double the displacement per unit $t$,
hence double the measured rate. To compare directions fairly — to ask "which *direction* is
steepest," not "which arrow is longest" — we will use **unit** directions, $\|u\| = 1$, so
that $t$ measures honest distance traveled. We flag where this matters as we go.

## The directional derivative is a dot product {#sec-dirderiv-dot}

We now connect @eq-dirderiv-def to the gradient. This is the bridge theorem: it says the
single directional derivative in *any* direction is recovered from the gradient by one dot
product. Everything geometric flows from it.

::: {#thm-dirderiv}
**Theorem (directional derivative via the gradient).** If $f:\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}$ has
continuous partial derivatives near $x$, then for any direction $u\in\mathbb{R}^n$,
$$
D_u f(x) = \nabla f(x)^\top u = \sum_{j=1}^n \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_j}(x)\,u_j .
$$ {#eq-dirderiv}
:::

::: {.proof}
We compute the limit in @eq-dirderiv-def directly, using the first-order approximation from
[GEOM-22]. With the nudge $\Delta x = tu$, @thm-firstorder of [GEOM-22] gives
$$
f(x + tu) = f(x) + \nabla f(x)^\top (tu) + r(tu),
$$
where the remainder satisfies $r(tu)/\|tu\| \to 0$ as $t \to 0$. Subtract $f(x)$ and divide
by $t$ (assume $t \neq 0$, since the limit is taken as $t\to 0$). Because the dot product is
linear, $\nabla f(x)^\top(tu) = t\,\nabla f(x)^\top u$, so the gradient term's $t$ cancels:
$$
\frac{f(x + tu) - f(x)}{t}
= \nabla f(x)^\top u + \frac{r(tu)}{t}.
$$
It remains to show the last term vanishes as $t \to 0$. Write it as
$$
\frac{r(tu)}{t} = \frac{r(tu)}{\|tu\|}\cdot\frac{\|tu\|}{t}
= \frac{r(tu)}{\|tu\|}\cdot \frac{|t|\,\|u\|}{t}.
$$
The factor $\frac{|t|\|u\|}{t} = \pm\|u\|$ is bounded (its magnitude is exactly $\|u\|$,
a fixed number), and the factor $\frac{r(tu)}{\|tu\|}\to 0$ by the remainder property of
@thm-firstorder, since $\|tu\|\to 0$ as $t\to 0$. A bounded quantity times a quantity going
to zero goes to zero. Hence
$$
D_u f(x) = \lim_{t\to 0}\frac{f(x+tu)-f(x)}{t} = \nabla f(x)^\top u,
$$
and writing out the dot product by its definition ([GEOM-04]) gives the component form.
$\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

Read what @eq-dirderiv accomplishes. We have $n$ slopes along the axes, the partials; the
theorem says the slope along *any* direction is a *linear combination* of those axis-slopes,
weighted by the components of $u$. Nothing about off-axis directions had to be measured
separately — the gradient already encodes all of them. This is the precise sense in which
the gradient "knows" the surface's first-order behaviour in every direction at once, and it
is why a single vector is enough.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Some textbooks define the directional derivative only for unit $u$, others for any $u$, and
some insist $D_u f = \nabla f^\top u$ holds 'by definition.' Which is it?"* The cleanest
story, and the one to give: $D_u f$ is defined by the *limit* @eq-dirderiv-def for *any*
$u$ — that is the honest definition, independent of the gradient. The formula $D_u f =
\nabla f^\top u$ is then a *theorem* (@thm-dirderiv) that requires the partials to be
*continuous* — exactly the differentiability condition flagged in [GEOM-22]. For badly
behaved functions the limit can exist for every $u$ yet *fail* to equal $\nabla f^\top u$
(the same pathology as partials-exist-but-not-differentiable from [GEOM-22]). The
restriction to unit $u$ is a separate convention used only when comparing steepness across
directions, so that $\|u\|$ does not inflate the number; it is not part of the definition.
Keeping these three things distinct — the limit definition, the dot-product theorem, the
unit-length convention — is what the question is testing.
:::

## Steepest ascent: the Cauchy–Schwarz argument {#sec-steepest}

Now the headline result. We have, for each unit direction $u$, a slope $D_u f =
\nabla f^\top u$. Which unit $u$ makes it largest? Write the dot product in its
geometric form from [GEOM-04]. For any two vectors, $a^\top b = \|a\|\,\|b\|\cos\theta$, where
$\theta$ is the angle between them. With $a = \nabla f$ and $b = u$, and using $\|u\| = 1$,
$$
D_u f = \nabla f^\top u = \|\nabla f\|\,\|u\|\cos\theta = \|\nabla f\|\cos\theta,
$$ {#eq-cosform}
where $\theta$ is the angle between the step direction $u$ and the gradient $\nabla f$. The
factor $\|\nabla f\|$ is fixed — it is a property of the point $x$, not of the direction we
choose. So the *only* thing we control by choosing $u$ is $\cos\theta$. And $\cos\theta$ is
largest, equal to $1$, exactly when $\theta = 0$ — when $u$ points in the *same direction* as
$\nabla f$. We have proved the result, but let us state it carefully and note the boundary.

::: {#thm-steepest}
**Theorem (steepest ascent).** Suppose $\nabla f(x) \neq 0$. Among all unit directions
$u$ ($\|u\|=1$), the directional derivative $D_u f(x) = \nabla f(x)^\top u$ is
**maximized** by
$$
u^\star = \frac{\nabla f(x)}{\|\nabla f(x)\|},
$$
the unit vector pointing along the gradient, and the maximum value is
$$
D_{u^\star} f(x) = \|\nabla f(x)\| .
$$
Symmetrically, the slope is **minimized** (steepest *descent*) by $u = -\nabla f/\|\nabla
f\|$, with value $-\|\nabla f\|$. In words: the gradient points the steepest way uphill, its
negative the steepest way downhill, and the length of the gradient is the steepest slope.
:::

::: {.proof}
We give two proofs; they illuminate the result from different sides, and an interviewer may
want either.

*Proof via the angle form.* From @eq-cosform, $D_u f = \|\nabla f\|\cos\theta$ for unit $u$.
Since $-1 \le \cos\theta \le 1$ for every angle, and $\|\nabla f\| > 0$ by assumption,
$$
-\|\nabla f\| \;\le\; D_u f \;\le\; \|\nabla f\| .
$$
The upper bound is attained iff $\cos\theta = 1$, i.e. $\theta = 0$, i.e. $u$ is the unit
vector along $\nabla f$, which is $u^\star = \nabla f/\|\nabla f\|$; then $D_{u^\star}f =
\|\nabla f\|\cdot 1 = \|\nabla f\|$. The lower bound is attained iff $\cos\theta = -1$, i.e.
$u = -\nabla f/\|\nabla f\|$, giving $-\|\nabla f\|$.

*Proof via Cauchy–Schwarz.* The **Cauchy–Schwarz inequality** ([GEOM-04]) states that for
any vectors $a,b$,
$$
|a^\top b| \le \|a\|\,\|b\|,
$$
with equality in the inequality **iff** $a$ and $b$ are parallel — that is, one is a scalar
multiple of the other, $b = \lambda a$ for some scalar $\lambda$ (or one of them is zero).
Apply it with $a = \nabla f$, $b = u$, $\|u\| = 1$:
$$
D_u f = \nabla f^\top u \le |\nabla f^\top u| \le \|\nabla f\|\,\|u\| = \|\nabla f\| .
$$
There are two "$\le$" signs here, and the upper bound $\|\nabla f\|$ is reached only when
*both* are equalities. The second, $|\nabla f^\top u| = \|\nabla f\|\,\|u\|$, is the
Cauchy–Schwarz equality case: it forces $u = \lambda\,\nabla f$ for some scalar $\lambda$.
The first, $\nabla f^\top u = |\nabla f^\top u|$, forces the dot product to be nonnegative,
which rules out $\lambda < 0$. With $u = \lambda\,\nabla f$ and $\|u\| = 1$ we get
$|\lambda|\,\|\nabla f\| = 1$, so $\lambda = 1/\|\nabla f\| > 0$ (taking the positive root,
as $\lambda \ge 0$), giving $u = \nabla f/\|\nabla f\| = u^\star$ and then $D_{u^\star}f =
\nabla f^\top u^\star = \|\nabla f\|^2/\|\nabla f\| = \|\nabla f\|$. The descent case is the
same with $\lambda = -1/\|\nabla f\|$, giving $-\|\nabla f\|$. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

This is the theorem that justifies the name. Notice what each piece of the gradient *means*
now, geometrically:

- **The direction of $\nabla f$** is the heading of steepest climb. Turn the wheel any other
  way and, by @eq-cosform, you give up a factor of $\cos\theta < 1$ of the available slope.
- **The length $\|\nabla f\|$** is the steepest slope itself — the actual rate of climb when
  you head straight uphill. A long gradient means a steep hill; a short one means gentle
  terrain; a zero gradient means flat in every direction (the subject of @sec-optimality).
- **The negative gradient** $-\nabla f$ is steepest *descent* — the direction Cauchy's 1847
  method steps, and the direction every gradient-descent optimizer follows. We will *only*
  point at the algorithm; building it is Optimization's job (@sec-descent-preview).

@fig-steepest makes the trade-off concrete: it draws the directional slope $D_u f$ as $u$
sweeps around the full circle, and you can watch it peak exactly when $u$ lines up with the
gradient.

```{python}
#| label: fig-steepest
#| fig-cap: "Steepest ascent, two views. **Left:** at a fixed point on the surface $f(x)=\\tfrac12 x_1^2 + 2x_2^2$, the gradient (red) is one specific arrow; three other unit step directions (grey) are shown with their slopes $D_u f = \\nabla f^\\top u$. **Right:** the directional slope $D_u f$ plotted against the heading angle $\\phi$ of $u$ as it sweeps the full circle. It traces a cosine, $\\|\\nabla f\\|\\cos(\\phi - \\phi_{\\nabla})$ — peaking at $+\\|\\nabla f\\|$ when $u$ aligns with $\\nabla f$ (red dot), hitting zero when $u$ is perpendicular (along the contour), and bottoming at $-\\|\\nabla f\\|$ for steepest descent. The whole of @thm-steepest is in that curve."
def fq(x):
    return 0.5 * x[0]**2 + 2.0 * x[1]**2
def gq(x):
    return np.array([x[0], 4.0 * x[1]])

p = np.array([1.4, 0.6])
g = gq(p)
gn = np.linalg.norm(g)
phi_grad = np.arctan2(g[1], g[0])

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.0, 4.0))

# Left: gradient and some sample directions, on the contour map
cg = np.linspace(-2.6, 2.6, 200)
CX, CY = np.meshgrid(cg, cg)
axL.contour(CX, CY, 0.5*CX**2 + 2.0*CY**2, levels=12, colors=PALE, linewidths=1.0)
gu = g / gn
draw_vec(axL, (p[0], p[1]), (p[0] + gu[0], p[1] + gu[1]), color=GRAD,
         label=r"$\nabla f$", label_offset=(0.0, 0.28))
for ang in (phi_grad + 0.9, phi_grad + 2.1, phi_grad - 1.4):
    u = np.array([np.cos(ang), np.sin(ang)])
    draw_vec(axL, (p[0], p[1]), (p[0] + u[0], p[1] + u[1]), color=GREY, lw=1.6)
axL.scatter([p[0]], [p[1]], color="black", s=30, zorder=6)
axL.set_aspect("equal"); axL.set_xlim(-2.6, 2.6); axL.set_ylim(-2.6, 2.6)
axL.set_xlabel("$x_1$"); axL.set_ylabel("$x_2$")
axL.set_title("one gradient, many step directions")

# Right: D_u f as u sweeps the circle
phis = np.linspace(-np.pi, np.pi, 400)
Du = np.array([g @ np.array([np.cos(ph), np.sin(ph)]) for ph in phis])
axR.plot(phis, Du, color=VEC)
axR.axhline(0, color=GREY, lw=1.0)
axR.scatter([phi_grad], [gn], color=GRAD, s=50, zorder=5)
axR.scatter([phi_grad - np.pi if phi_grad > 0 else phi_grad + np.pi], [-gn],
            color=ACCENT, s=50, zorder=5)
axR.set_xlabel(r"heading angle $\phi$ of $u$"); axR.set_ylabel(r"$D_u f = \nabla f^\top u$")
axR.set_title(r"slope peaks when $u \parallel \nabla f$")
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Is the negative gradient the fastest route to the minimum?"* A favourite trap. No — it is
the *locally* steepest descent direction, the one that decreases $f$ fastest *for an
infinitesimal step at the current point*. It is **not** generally the direction that points
at the minimum, and following it can zig-zag badly when the surface is stretched (an
elongated bowl), taking many steps to reach the bottom. The reason is exactly the curvature
the *Hessian* measures ([GEOM-24]) and the *conditioning* the condition number measures
([GEOM-26]): steepest descent is blind to how the steepness *changes* as you move. Methods
that use that second-order information — Newton's method, conjugate gradients — can aim much
closer to the true minimum. The gradient gives the best *direction* under a first-order
(linear) view of the world; that view is local, and its limits are the whole motivation for
the articles that follow.
:::

## The gradient is perpendicular to the level set {#sec-perp}

The second geometric fact, and the one that makes contour maps legible. A **level set** of
$f$ at value $c$ is the set of inputs where $f$ equals $c$:
$$
\{\,x : f(x) = c\,\},
$$
the contour of constant height — an elevation line on a topographic map, a constant-loss
curve in parameter space. The claim is that $\nabla f$ is orthogonal to this set at every
point: it makes a right angle with every direction you could travel *along* the contour.

The intuition is already in hand from @eq-cosform. Moving *along* a contour means $f$ does
not change, so the directional derivative in that direction is zero. But $D_u f = \nabla
f^\top u$, so a zero directional derivative means $\nabla f^\top u = 0$ — the gradient is
perpendicular to that direction by the dot-product definition of orthogonality ([GEOM-05]).
We make this precise with a curve along the contour.

::: {#thm-perp}
**Theorem (gradient $\perp$ level set).** Let $\gamma(t)$ be any smooth curve that lies
inside a level set of $f$ — that is, $f(\gamma(t)) = c$ for all $t$, with constant $c$ — and
suppose $\gamma(0) = x$. Then the gradient at $x$ is orthogonal to the curve's velocity
(tangent) vector there:
$$
\nabla f(x)^\top \gamma'(0) = 0 .
$$
Since this holds for *every* such curve, $\nabla f(x)$ is orthogonal to every direction
tangent to the level set at $x$ — i.e. $\nabla f(x)$ is perpendicular to the level set.
:::

::: {.proof}
Define the one-variable function $\psi(t) = f(\gamma(t))$, the height of $f$ as we travel
along the curve. By hypothesis $\gamma$ stays in the level set, so $f(\gamma(t)) = c$ for all
$t$: $\psi$ is the *constant* function $c$. The derivative of a constant is zero, so
$\psi'(0) = 0$.

Now compute $\psi'(0)$ a second way, with the chain rule. $\psi = f\circ\gamma$ is a scalar
function $f$ composed with the vector-valued path $\gamma$, exactly the setting of the chain
rule from [GEOM-22] (@thm-chain there). The Jacobian of the path $\gamma:\mathbb{R}\to
\mathbb{R}^n$ is the column of derivatives $\gamma'(t)$ (one output coordinate at a time,
each an ordinary Calc-1 derivative), so the chain rule gives
$$
\psi'(t) = \nabla f(\gamma(t))^\top \gamma'(t) .
$$
Concretely, this is the multivariable chain rule written out: $\frac{d}{dt}f(\gamma(t)) =
\sum_{j=1}^n \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_j}(\gamma(t))\,\gamma_j'(t)$, the sum of $f$'s
change through each coordinate of the moving point — which is precisely the dot product
$\nabla f(\gamma(t))^\top\gamma'(t)$ ([GEOM-04]). Evaluate at $t=0$, where $\gamma(0)=x$:
$$
\psi'(0) = \nabla f(x)^\top \gamma'(0) .
$$
Setting the two computations of $\psi'(0)$ equal — zero from the constant, the dot product
from the chain rule — gives $\nabla f(x)^\top\gamma'(0) = 0$. The tangent vector $\gamma'(0)$
can be any direction tangent to the level set (choose the curve to head whichever way you
like along the contour), so $\nabla f(x)$ is orthogonal to all of them. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

This is the surveyor's fact, now a theorem: **the steepest direction crosses the lines of no
change at a right angle.** It also re-explains the steepest-ascent result from a second
angle. If the gradient were *not* perpendicular to the contour, it would have a nonzero
component *along* the contour — a component in a direction where $f$ does not change, which
would be wasted effort for climbing. The steepest climb spends all its motion crossing
contours, none of it sliding along them, which is exactly "perpendicular to the level set."
@fig-perp shows the gradient field draped over the contours so the right angles are visible
everywhere.

```{python}
#| label: fig-perp
#| fig-cap: "The gradient field is everywhere perpendicular to the level sets. For $f(x)=x_1^2 + 3x_2^2$ (an elliptical bowl), the pale curves are level sets (constant-$f$ ellipses) and each red arrow is the gradient $\\nabla f = (2x_1, 6x_2)$ at its base point. Every arrow meets its contour at a right angle and points 'outward' toward higher values — the visual statement of both theorems at once: steepest ascent (outward, uphill) and orthogonality (square to the contour). Note the arrows are longer where the contours bunch together (steeper terrain, larger $\\|\\nabla f\\|$)."
cg = np.linspace(-2.6, 2.6, 300)
CX, CY = np.meshgrid(cg, cg)
F = CX**2 + 3.0*CY**2
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.0, 5.0))
ax.contour(CX, CY, F, levels=np.arange(1, 25, 2.5), colors=PALE, linewidths=1.0)
gg = np.linspace(-2.2, 2.2, 9)
GXp, GYp = np.meshgrid(gg, gg)
U = 2.0 * GXp
V = 6.0 * GYp
ax.quiver(GXp, GYp, U, V, color=GRAD, angles="xy", scale_units="xy", scale=18,
          width=0.005)
ax.set_aspect("equal"); ax.set_xlim(-2.6, 2.6); ax.set_ylim(-2.6, 2.6)
ax.set_xlabel("$x_1$"); ax.set_ylabel("$x_2$")
ax.set_title(r"$\nabla f \perp$ level sets, pointing uphill")
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Does 'gradient is perpendicular to the level set' work in higher dimensions, where I can't
draw a contour?"* Yes, and the proof above already covers it — nowhere did we use $n=2$. In
$\mathbb{R}^n$ a level set $\{x: f(x)=c\}$ is generically an $(n-1)$-dimensional surface (a
*hypersurface*); its tangent directions at $x$ form an $(n-1)$-dimensional space, and
@thm-perp says $\nabla f(x)$ is orthogonal to *all* of them. So the gradient spans the *one*
direction left over — the single direction not tangent to the level set, the normal to the
hypersurface. This is the engine behind the method of Lagrange multipliers (where a
constrained optimum forces the objective's gradient to be parallel to the constraint's
gradient, because both must be normal to the same constraint surface), which the
Optimization track builds on this fact.
:::

## First-order optimality: where the gradient vanishes {#sec-optimality}

We can now read off the condition for a maximum or minimum — the multivariable analogue of
"set the derivative to zero" from Calc 1, and the condition every optimization method is
ultimately solving.

Suppose $x^\star$ is a **local minimum** of $f$: $f(x^\star) \le f(x)$ for all $x$ near
$x^\star$. Could the gradient there be nonzero? If $\nabla f(x^\star) \neq 0$, then by
@thm-steepest the direction $u = -\nabla f/\|\nabla f\|$ has directional derivative
$D_u f(x^\star) = -\|\nabla f(x^\star)\| < 0$ — a *strictly negative* slope. A negative slope
means $f$ *decreases* as we step a little in that direction: there are points arbitrarily
close to $x^\star$ with smaller $f$. That contradicts $x^\star$ being a local minimum. So the
gradient cannot be nonzero, which leaves only one possibility.

::: {#thm-firstorder-opt}
**Theorem (first-order necessary condition).** If $f:\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}$ has
continuous partials and $x^\star$ is a local minimum or a local maximum of $f$, then
$$
\boxed{\;\nabla f(x^\star) = 0.\;}
$$
A point where $\nabla f = 0$ is called a **critical point** (or stationary point).
:::

::: {.proof}
Take the minimum case (the maximum is identical with inequalities reversed, or apply the
minimum argument to $-f$). Suppose for contradiction $\nabla f(x^\star) \neq 0$. By
@thm-steepest the steepest-descent direction $u = -\nabla f(x^\star)/\|\nabla f(x^\star)\|$
satisfies $D_u f(x^\star) = -\|\nabla f(x^\star)\| < 0$. By definition @eq-dirderiv-def this
directional derivative is the *two-sided* limit $D_u f(x^\star) = \lim_{t\to 0}\frac{f(x^\star
+ tu) - f(x^\star)}{t}$; whenever a two-sided limit exists and equals $L$, the one-sided limit
from the right equals the same $L$, so $\lim_{t\to 0^+}\frac{f(x^\star + tu) - f(x^\star)}{t} =
-\|\nabla f(x^\star)\| < 0$. A limit from the right being a negative number means that for all
sufficiently small $t > 0$ the difference quotient $\frac{f(x^\star+tu)-f(x^\star)}{t}$ is
itself negative; since its denominator $t>0$, the numerator must be negative too, i.e.
$f(x^\star + tu) < f(x^\star)$. Those are
points arbitrarily close to $x^\star$ with strictly smaller value, contradicting that
$x^\star$ is a local minimum. Therefore $\nabla f(x^\star) = 0$. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

Two cautions, both essential, both the reason the *next* article exists.

**The condition is necessary, not sufficient.** $\nabla f = 0$ says "no direction is uphill
*and* no direction is downhill, to first order" — the surface is locally flat. That is true at
a minimum, but equally true at a *maximum* and at a *saddle point* (uphill in one direction,
downhill in another, like a mountain pass). First-order information cannot tell these apart;
all three are critical points. @fig-critical draws the three. Deciding *which* one a critical
point is requires the *second* derivatives — the Hessian — which is precisely [GEOM-24].

**It is a local statement.** $\nabla f = 0$ detects local flatness; it says nothing about
whether $x^\star$ is the *global* best. A function can have many critical points, and finding
the global minimum among them is a separate, generally hard problem — unless the surface is a
single convex bowl (such as a quadratic form with positive-definite matrix, [GEOM-25],
[GEOM-18]), in which case the unique critical point *is* the global minimum. That special,
beautiful case is the convergence point of this whole movement.

```{python}
#| label: fig-critical
#| fig-cap: "Three critical points, all with $\\nabla f = 0$, all looking flat to first order. **Left:** a minimum, $f=x_1^2+x_2^2$ (a bowl). **Middle:** a maximum, $f=-(x_1^2+x_2^2)$ (a dome). **Right:** a saddle, $f=x_1^2 - x_2^2$ (uphill along $x_1$, downhill along $x_2$). At the marked centre point the gradient vanishes in all three — the first-order condition cannot distinguish them. Telling them apart needs curvature (the Hessian, GEOM-24)."
fig = plt.figure(figsize=(9.6, 3.4))
g = np.linspace(-1.6, 1.6, 60)
GX, GY = np.meshgrid(g, g)
surfs = [("minimum  $x_1^2+x_2^2$", GX**2 + GY**2),
         ("maximum  $-(x_1^2+x_2^2)$", -(GX**2 + GY**2)),
         ("saddle  $x_1^2-x_2^2$", GX**2 - GY**2)]
for k, (title, Zk) in enumerate(surfs, start=1):
    axk = fig.add_subplot(1, 3, k, projection="3d")
    axk.plot_surface(GX, GY, Zk, cmap="viridis", alpha=0.85, linewidth=0, antialiased=True)
    axk.scatter([0], [0], [0], color=GRAD, s=40, depthshade=False)
    axk.set_title(title, fontsize=10)
    axk.set_xlabel("$x_1$"); axk.set_ylabel("$x_2$")
    axk.view_init(elev=28, azim=-130)
plt.tight_layout()
```

## Gradient descent, named but not built {#sec-descent-preview}

We owe the reader an honest signpost. The negative gradient is the steepest-descent direction
(@thm-steepest), so the natural way to *minimize* $f$ is Cauchy's idea: start somewhere, step
a little against the gradient, repeat,
$$
x_{k+1} = x_k - \eta\,\nabla f(x_k),
$$
with a step size $\eta > 0$. This is **gradient descent**. We state it *only* to show where
the geometry leads — and then we stop, deliberately. The questions that make it an
*algorithm* — how big should $\eta$ be, when does the iteration converge and how fast, why
does it zig-zag on stretched bowls, what to do about saddle points, momentum, line search —
are **Optimization's** to answer, building directly on this article's geometry and on the
conditioning story of [GEOM-26]. The boundary is the track's ownership rule: *Geometry owns
the gradient as a direction; Optimization owns the iteration that walks along it.* What this
article guarantees is the single fact the algorithm rests on — that $-\nabla f$ is the
locally fastest way down — and the caution that "locally fastest" is not "globally smartest,"
which is why Optimization needs more than this article gives.

# Worked examples {#sec-examples}

We now run the geometry on honest functions, verifying the steepest direction *numerically*
against the analytic gradient — the finite-difference check is the discrete shadow of
@eq-dirderiv-def, the same bridge used in [GEOM-22].

## Example 1 — a real loss surface: logistic-regression negative log-likelihood {#sec-ex-logistic}

A genuine machine-learning loss, not a toy. Logistic regression fits a probability
$\sigma(w^\top x_i) = 1/(1+e^{-w^\top x_i})$ to binary labels $y_i\in\{0,1\}$ by minimizing
the average negative log-likelihood. With a two-dimensional weight vector $w=(w_1,w_2)$ we
can see the whole surface. Its gradient has the clean closed form
$\nabla_w L = \tfrac1m\sum_i (\sigma(w^\top x_i) - y_i)\,x_i$ (derived in the Classification
track; here we use it and *check* it). We confirm the steepest-ascent claim directly: the
analytic gradient should beat every other unit direction's directional slope, and the
finite-difference gradient should match it.

```{python}
#| label: tbl-logistic-grad
#| echo: true
import numpy as np
rng = np.random.default_rng(1)

# A small 2-feature binary dataset.
m = 200
X = rng.normal(size=(m, 2))
w_true = np.array([1.3, -0.8])
p = 1.0 / (1.0 + np.exp(-(X @ w_true)))
y = (rng.uniform(size=m) < p).astype(float)

def sigmoid(z):
    return 1.0 / (1.0 + np.exp(-z))

def loss(w):
    z = X @ w
    # mean negative log-likelihood, written stably
    return np.mean(np.logaddexp(0.0, z) - y * z)

def grad_analytic(w):
    return X.T @ (sigmoid(X @ w) - y) / m

w0 = np.array([0.2, 0.4])               # a point on the surface
g = grad_analytic(w0)

# finite-difference gradient: the discrete shadow of the directional-derivative limit
def fd_grad(fn, w, h=1e-6):
    out = np.zeros_like(w)
    for j in range(len(w)):
        e = np.zeros_like(w); e[j] = h
        out[j] = (fn(w + e) - fn(w - e)) / (2 * h)
    return out

g_fd = fd_grad(loss, w0)
print("analytic gradient            :", np.round(g, 6))
print("finite-difference gradient   :", np.round(g_fd, 6))
print("max abs difference           :", np.max(np.abs(g - g_fd)))

# Steepest-ascent check: D_u f for the gradient direction vs many random unit directions.
u_grad = g / np.linalg.norm(g)
def dir_deriv(fn, w, u, h=1e-6):
    return (fn(w + h * u) - fn(w - h * u)) / (2 * h)

slope_grad = dir_deriv(loss, w0, u_grad)
rand_slopes = []
for _ in range(8):
    v = rng.normal(size=2); v /= np.linalg.norm(v)
    rand_slopes.append(dir_deriv(loss, w0, v))
print("\nD_u f along +gradient (steepest) :", round(slope_grad, 6),
      "   = ||grad|| =", round(np.linalg.norm(g), 6))
print("D_u f along 8 random directions  :", np.round(rand_slopes, 4))
print("is the gradient direction the steepest? ",
      all(slope_grad >= s - 1e-9 for s in rand_slopes))
```

Three things the printout confirms operationally. First, the analytic gradient matches the
finite-difference gradient to the step precision — the closed-form $\nabla_w L$ is exactly
@def-partial made discrete. Second, the directional slope *along the gradient direction*
equals $\|\nabla f\|$, the number @thm-steepest predicts for the steepest direction. Third,
no random unit direction beats it — the gradient is the steepest, as the theorem demands.
@fig-logistic draws the surface's contours, the gradient arrow, and a steepest-descent path
that visibly crosses the contours at right angles on its way to the minimum.

```{python}
#| label: fig-logistic
#| fig-cap: "The logistic-regression loss surface over the two weights. Contours are constant-loss curves; the red arrow at the start point is $\\nabla L$, pointing uphill and perpendicular to its contour. The green path is steepest descent ($w \\gets w - \\eta\\nabla L$, Optimization's algorithm, shown here only to illustrate the geometry): each step heads along $-\\nabla L$, square to the local contour, walking the surface down toward the minimum (green dot). The loss is a single convex bowl, so the one critical point is the global minimum — the well-behaved case of @sec-optimality."
w1g = np.linspace(-1.0, 3.0, 120)
w2g = np.linspace(-2.5, 1.5, 120)
W1, W2 = np.meshgrid(w1g, w2g)
Ls = np.zeros_like(W1)
for i in range(W1.shape[0]):
    for j in range(W1.shape[1]):
        Ls[i, j] = loss(np.array([W1[i, j], W2[i, j]]))

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.2, 5.0))
ax.contour(W1, W2, Ls, levels=18, colors=PALE, linewidths=1.0)

# steepest-descent path (geometry illustration only)
w = np.array([0.2, 0.4]); eta = 1.6
path = [w.copy()]
for _ in range(40):
    w = w - eta * grad_analytic(w)
    path.append(w.copy())
path = np.array(path)
ax.plot(path[:, 0], path[:, 1], color=ACCENT, lw=1.8, marker="o", ms=3,
        label="steepest-descent path")

# gradient arrow at the start
w0 = np.array([0.2, 0.4]); g0 = grad_analytic(w0)
g0u = g0 / np.linalg.norm(g0) * 0.6
draw_vec(ax, (w0[0], w0[1]), (w0[0] + g0u[0], w0[1] + g0u[1]), color=GRAD,
         label=r"$\nabla L$", label_offset=(0.18, 0.18))
ax.scatter([path[-1, 0]], [path[-1, 1]], color=ACCENT, s=80, zorder=6,
           edgecolor="white", label="minimum")
ax.set_xlabel(r"$w_1$"); ax.set_ylabel(r"$w_2$")
ax.set_title(r"$-\nabla L$ steps cross contours at right angles")
ax.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

## Example 2 — the gradient of a quadratic form, where steepest is not toward the minimum {#sec-ex-quad}

The honest, near-the-edge example. Steepest descent is *locally* fastest but can be a poor
*global* route, and a stretched quadratic bowl is where this bites hardest — a preview of why
[GEOM-24] and [GEOM-26] exist. Take the quadratic form $f(x) = \tfrac12 x^\top A x$ with
$A = \mathrm{diag}(1, 20)$ — a valley that is twenty times steeper across than along. Its
gradient is $\nabla f = Ax$ (the quadratic-form identity from [GEOM-22], finished in
[GEOM-25]). The minimum is at the origin. We show that the steepest-descent direction at a
typical point does *not* point at the origin, and the resulting path zig-zags.

```{python}
#| label: tbl-quad-zigzag
#| echo: true
import numpy as np
A = np.diag([1.0, 20.0])            # an ill-conditioned (stretched) bowl

def fq(x):  return 0.5 * x @ A @ x
def gq(x):  return A @ x            # gradient of (1/2) x^T A x  (GEOM-22 / GEOM-25)

x0 = np.array([9.0, 1.0])           # a starting point off the long axis
g0 = gq(x0)
dir_to_min = -x0 / np.linalg.norm(x0)        # the direction that points AT the minimum
dir_steepest = -g0 / np.linalg.norm(g0)      # the steepest-descent direction
cos_angle = dir_to_min @ dir_steepest        # 1 would mean "steepest points at the min"
print("steepest-descent direction :", np.round(dir_steepest, 4))
print("direction toward minimum   :", np.round(dir_to_min, 4))
print("cos(angle between them)    :", round(cos_angle, 4),
      "  -> they are NOT the same direction")
```

The two directions disagree: the steepest-descent heading is dominated by the steep
$x_2$-axis and points well off the line to the minimum. @fig-zigzag shows the consequence —
steepest descent ricochets between the steep walls, taking many small steps, while the
straight line to the minimum is right there. This is not a flaw in the gradient; it is the
honest *limit* of first-order geometry. The gradient correctly reports the locally steepest
direction; it simply cannot see that the bowl is stretched, because stretch is *curvature*,
a second-derivative property. Recognizing this is the bridge to the rest of the movement.

```{python}
#| label: fig-zigzag
#| fig-cap: "The edge of the gradient's usefulness. The bowl $f=\\tfrac12(x_1^2 + 20x_2^2)$ is stretched 20:1, so its contours are long thin ellipses. Steepest descent (green) repeatedly heads almost straight across the narrow valley, overshoots, and bounces — a slow zig-zag to the origin. The dashed grey line is the straight path to the minimum the gradient never takes. The gradient is locally correct at every step (always $\\perp$ the contour, always steepest), yet globally inefficient here: 'steepest' is not 'smartest.' Curvature (GEOM-24) and conditioning (GEOM-26) explain and fix this."
cg1 = np.linspace(-10, 10, 300)
cg2 = np.linspace(-3, 3, 300)
CX, CY = np.meshgrid(cg1, cg2)
F = 0.5 * (CX**2 + 20.0 * CY**2)
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.4, 3.6))
ax.contour(CX, CY, F, levels=np.linspace(2, 120, 16), colors=PALE, linewidths=1.0)

x = np.array([9.0, 1.0]); eta = 0.09
path = [x.copy()]
for _ in range(60):
    x = x - eta * gq(x)
    path.append(x.copy())
path = np.array(path)
ax.plot(path[:, 0], path[:, 1], color=ACCENT, lw=1.6, marker="o", ms=2.5,
        label="steepest descent (zig-zag)")
ax.plot([9.0, 0.0], [1.0, 0.0], color=GREY, ls="--", lw=1.4,
        label="straight line to minimum")
ax.scatter([0], [0], color=GRAD, s=70, zorder=6, edgecolor="white", label="minimum")
ax.set_aspect("equal"); ax.set_xlim(-10, 10); ax.set_ylim(-3, 3)
ax.set_xlabel("$x_1$"); ax.set_ylabel("$x_2$")
ax.set_title("locally steepest, globally slow")
ax.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

# Failure modes and the edge of applicability {#sec-failure}

The geometry of this article is exact wherever the gradient exists and is continuous, but
there are honest boundaries — the places where "follow the gradient" is the wrong instinct.

## The gradient does not exist: kinks and corners {#sec-nondiff}

Everything here assumed continuous partials, so that @thm-dirderiv and @thm-firstorder of
[GEOM-22] apply. Several important losses violate that. The absolute-value loss $|r|$ (least
absolute deviations), the hinge loss of an SVM, the ReLU activation $\max(0,z)$, and the
$\ell_1$ penalty $\|w\|_1$ all have *kinks* where the left and right slopes disagree, so the
limit @eq-dirderiv-def does not exist and there is no gradient to point along. At such a
point "steepest ascent" is undefined — there is a *fan* of candidate slopes, not one. The
correct generalization is the **subgradient**, the set of all linear under-estimators, which
the Optimization track develops (and [GEOM-22] flagged in its own failure section). Here the
honest statement is: the steepest-direction theorem is a *smooth* theorem; at a corner it has
nothing to say, and you switch tools.

## A critical point is not a minimum: the saddle trap {#sec-saddle}

The single most common misuse of $\nabla f = 0$ is to read it as "we found the minimum." We
proved only that it is *necessary* (@thm-firstorder-opt): minima, maxima, and saddles all
satisfy it. In high dimensions saddle points vastly outnumber local minima for typical
non-convex losses (a deep-network loss surface is riddled with them), so a solver that merely
drives $\nabla f$ to zero can stall at a saddle that is *not* a minimum at all. **What you
reach for instead:** the second-order test using the Hessian's definiteness ([GEOM-24],
[GEOM-18]) — positive-definite Hessian means a genuine local minimum, indefinite means a
saddle. First-order geometry locates the *candidates*; second-order geometry classifies them.
This is the entire reason the next article exists, and we hand off to it cleanly.

## Vanishing and exploding gradients: the magnitude misleads {#sec-vanishing}

Because $\|\nabla f\|$ is the rate of climb, a *tiny* gradient signals nearly flat terrain —
which can be a true minimum, but can equally be a long flat plateau or a saddle's flat
direction, where progress stalls without any optimum nearby. The infamous *vanishing
gradient* problem in deep networks is exactly this: gradients shrink to near-zero through
many layers, the steepest direction becomes a near-zero arrow, and learning grinds to a halt
even though no good solution has been reached. Conversely *exploding* gradients (huge
$\|\nabla f\|$) make steepest-descent steps overshoot wildly. The lesson for this article:
the gradient's *direction* is geometrically meaningful at every point it exists, but its
*magnitude* is a slope, not a distance — a small or large $\|\nabla f\|$ tells you the local
steepness, never how far the minimum is. Reading magnitude as "closeness to the answer" is a
genuine error.

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**Three things $\nabla f = 0$ does *not* tell you.** (1) It does not tell you the point is a
minimum — it could be a maximum or a saddle (@sec-saddle); you need the Hessian. (2) It does
not tell you the point is the *global* optimum — only convexity does that ([GEOM-18],
[GEOM-25]). (3) And a *small but nonzero* $\|\nabla f\|$ does not tell you the minimum is
near — it tells you the terrain is locally gentle, which is true on plateaus and near saddles
too. The gradient is a first-order, local instrument; treating it as a global verdict is the
recurring trap.
:::

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"When is following the gradient the right thing to do, and when is it not?"* The honest
summary to recite:

- **Trust the gradient's direction** whenever $f$ is differentiable: $-\nabla f$ is provably
  the locally steepest descent (@thm-steepest), and $\nabla f = 0$ is the right *necessary*
  condition to chase (@thm-firstorder-opt). On a convex bowl (a positive-definite quadratic,
  [GEOM-25]) that is the whole story — one critical point, the global minimum.
- **Do not rely on it alone** when the surface is curved unevenly (steepest descent zig-zags,
  @sec-ex-quad — use curvature, [GEOM-24], or preconditioning, [GEOM-26]), when there are
  many critical points (saddles and local minima, non-convex losses — needs the Hessian and
  good initialization), or when $f$ is non-smooth (kinks — use subgradients).
- **And never confuse the magnitude with proximity:** $\|\nabla f\|$ is a slope, not a
  remaining distance to the optimum.
:::

# Connections and where to go next {#sec-connections}

::: {.callout-important title="Where you are in the track"}
You arrived from **[GEOM-22] *Vector and Matrix Differentiation***, which built the gradient
as a stacked column of partial derivatives and proved the first-order approximation
$f(x+\Delta x)\approx f(x)+\nabla f^\top\Delta x$. This article turned that algebraic object
into a *geometric* one, using the dot-product geometry of **[GEOM-04]** (the angle form
$u^\top v=\|u\|\|v\|\cos\theta$ and Cauchy–Schwarz) and the orthogonality of **[GEOM-05]**:
the gradient is the steepest-ascent direction, its length is the steepest slope, it is
perpendicular to the level sets, and it vanishes at every critical point. From here Movement
VI finishes:

- **[GEOM-24] — The Hessian and the Curvature of a Surface.** We found *where* the surface is
  flat ($\nabla f = 0$); next we differentiate the gradient again to get the matrix of second
  partials, whose definiteness ([GEOM-18]) finally tells us *which kind* of critical point we
  are at — minimum, maximum, or saddle. The saddle trap of @sec-saddle is resolved there.
- **[GEOM-25] — The Gradient and Hessian of a Quadratic Form.** The convergence point of the
  movement: for $f(x)=\tfrac12 x^\top A x - b^\top x$ the gradient is $Ax - b$ and setting it
  to zero gives $Ax = b$ — so minimizing a quadratic *is* solving a linear system, with a
  unique global minimum exactly when $A$ is positive-definite. The stretched-bowl example of
  @sec-ex-quad is exactly that surface, and its conditioning is [GEOM-26].

Downstream of the track, this article is the **geometric foundation the Optimization track
builds gradient descent on** — the $x_{k+1}=x_k-\eta\nabla f(x_k)$ step of @sec-descent-preview
is theirs to analyze, consuming the steepest-descent theorem and the conditioning story of
[GEOM-26] and linking back here rather than re-deriving the geometry. It also supplies the
**first-order critical-point condition $\nabla\ell(\theta)=0$** that Inference's score-equation
work ([INFR-B2]) stands on.
:::

# Going deeper {#sec-deeper}

- **The primary source.** A.-L. Cauchy, "Méthode générale pour la résolution des systèmes
  d'équations simultanées," *Comptes Rendus de l'Académie des Sciences* (1847) — two pages
  that introduce steepest descent and rest entirely on the steepest-direction fact this
  article proves.
- **The geometry, told well.** The directional-derivative / gradient / level-set picture is
  the heart of any vector-calculus text; for the data-science framing, the optimization
  chapters of Boyd & Vandenberghe, *Convex Optimization* (2004), connect the steepest
  direction and the descent step at exactly the boundary this track draws with Optimization.
- **The honest limits.** The zig-zag of @sec-ex-quad and the role of curvature are the
  motivation for Nocedal & Wright, *Numerical Optimization* (2006), whose early chapters
  contrast steepest descent with Newton and quasi-Newton methods — the second-order successors
  to this first-order article.
- **Open threads this article deliberately deferred.** *Which kind of critical point is it?*
  — the Hessian of [GEOM-24]. *Why does steepest descent zig-zag, and what fixes it?* — the
  conditioning of [GEOM-26]. *What is the steepest direction when $f$ is constrained?* — the
  normal-to-the-constraint-surface reading of @thm-perp, which becomes Lagrange multipliers in
  the Optimization track. *What replaces the gradient at a kink?* — the subgradient, also
  Optimization's.
